doc.title: Stock dividends — small vs large
doc.event: Instead of cash, the board declares a dividend paid in the company's own shares. 100,000 shares ($1 par) are outstanding, trading at $15. The size of the dividend flips the measurement rule: a SMALL stock dividend capitalizes fair value; a LARGE one capitalizes only par.

variants.label: 10% stock dividend — 10,000 new shares at the $15 market price

lines.label: 10,000 × $15 FV
lines.why: A small dividend barely moves the stock price, so accounting pretends shareholders received $15 shares — retained earnings capitalizes the full market value.
lines.trap: Small → FAIR value, large → PAR. Flip the rule and every number on the page is wrong.

lines.label: 10,000 × $1 par
lines.why: Par value of the shares owed — an EQUITY account (shares to be issued), not a liability.
lines.trap: 'Distributable' is not 'Payable.' No cash will ever leave; this sits in paid-in capital until the shares issue.

lines.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common
lines.label: the FV excess
lines.why: The difference between fair value capitalized and par distributable lands in PIC — permanent capital now.
lines.trap: Total equity does NOT change — this is a transfer from earned capital (RE) to contributed capital. No shareholder got richer.

lines.label: clear it
lines.why: The distributable account empties as the shares actually issue.
lines.trap: Only the par piece moves here — the PIC from declaration is already home.

lines.label: 10,000 shares now issued
lines.why: Shares outstanding: 110,000. Each shareholder owns the same fraction of the same company as before.
lines.trap: That's the punchline worth saying out loud: a stock dividend gives shareholders nothing but more pieces of paper representing the same pie.

computationPaths.narration: Small dividend arithmetic: new shares = 10% × 100,000 = 10,000; capitalize at FV (10,000 × $15 = 150,000); split par (10,000) vs excess (140,000).

variants.label: 50% stock dividend — 50,000 new shares at $1 PAR only

lines.label: 50,000 × $1 par
lines.why: A 50% dividend WILL crater the per-share price (it's economically split-like), so pretending shareholders got $15 value would be fiction — only par capitalizes.
lines.trap: Capitalizing 50,000 shares at FV (750,000) is the flip-the-rule disaster: fifteen times the correct RE hit.

lines.label: 50,000 × $1 par
lines.why: Par in, par out — a large dividend touches no PIC at all.
lines.trap: Two lines, equal amounts. If a third line appeared in your entry, you used the small-dividend rule.

memorize.body: Small (< ~20-25% of outstanding) → capitalize FAIR VALUE (RE debit = shares × market). Large → capitalize PAR (RE debit = shares × par). Both leave TOTAL equity unchanged.

A stock SPLIT is not a dividend and gets NO JOURNAL ENTRY AT ALL — memo only. 2-for-1: shares double, par halves, every account balance untouched. Writing an entry for a split is a free point handed back.
Dividend vs split tell: does par change? Split changes PAR per share (halves it); a stock dividend keeps par and changes RETAINED EARNINGS. One word in the problem decides everything.

questions.prompt: 100,000 shares ($1 par, $15 market) are outstanding; a 50% stock dividend is declared. Retained Earnings is debited for…

distractors.feedback: That capitalizes 50,000 shares at the $15 FAIR value — the SMALL-dividend rule. A 50% dividend is large: par only, 50,000 × $1 = $50,000.
Zero is the SPLIT answer (memo only). A 50% stock DIVIDEND is still a dividend — it capitalizes retained earnings at par.

accountBank.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common

build.scaffold: First call the size: under ~20-25% is small (fair value), over is large (par). Small = three-line declaration (RE at FV, distributable at par, PIC the gap). Large = two lines at par. And if the problem says SPLIT — put the pencil down; there's no entry.
